displayTitle: 2025-09-15 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill [Subcommittee B, Part 2]
topic: Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill
description: Monday 15 September 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill Westpac New Zealand Catherine McGrath, Chief Executive Sarah Walters Eamon Frazer Christopher Hawkins Julie Smith Fred Overmars Carillon (Caril) Cowan Dr. Debbie Hager Werner Geuther Federated Farmers of New Zealand Mark Hooper, Board member Federated Farmers of NZ Oliver Ibbetson, Government Relations Manager
descriptionSummary: Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill. Westpac New Zealand. Catherine McGrath, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Questions at the end if you'd like in your hands. Good morning and thank you for the opportunity to present on the bill. If the committee's amenable, I'll open with a short statement before moving straight to questions. So we take our submission as read, noting it should be considered alongside the NZBA submission as well.

We fully understand the intent of the bill, which seeks to ensure that people and businesses retain access to banking services and that these are not withdrawn for arbitrary reasons and we wholeheartedly support that sentiment. Where we differ from the bill is the characterization of ESG assessments as moralizing, when in reality these assessments are a part of prudent risk management and commercial decision making on a case by case basis.

Equally, we do not see evidence of the systemic harm this bill is looking to fix. Very happy to take questions. Thanks ya. That was very succinct. Um, members, are there any questions on the Westpac submission? Uh, Andy Foster. Well thanks. Thank you, Catherine.

Thank you for being so succinct with that gives us a bit of time to ask, ask some questions. Um, look, the first one of 'em, um, thank you very much for, um, the work that you're doing in terms of access around the, um, sort of more disadvantaged part of the community. So I really appreciated that. Um, what you said is you supported the intention, um, of trying to make sure that everybody's got access, reasonable access to financial services.

So thank you for that. Um, where though, where the question I think we're wrestling with is where is the balance between making a decision, uh, thinking, thinking in terms of industries, a decision, uh, about a, an industry being, say a, a quite sunset industry, um, and that becoming a self-fulfilling prophecy.

In other words, if, if banks, uh, one or more banks start with drawing services for a particular, uh, industry particular, um, groups of organizations, doesn't that make it a self-fulfilling pro? They then become a sunset industry because become becomes increasingly difficult for 'em to operate, more expensive to reach, to get financial services and so on.

How do we get that balance right, because I'm hearing you don't like the wording of the bill, but not necessarily post the intention of saying we that financial services are really important to, to get and how do we do that? So I think the first thing that's important to distinguish is between, uh, banking services is in having a bank account and lending.

So for banking services, uh, we attempt to bank, uh, everybody that we can. There are broadly two exceptions to that. One, where we are concerned about our anti-money laundering, uh, requirements or secondly where we are concerned about the health and safety of our staff. Um, and each of those are looked at on a case by case basis, lending decisions.

It's really important that they're commercial. Um, and they, there is a variety of, so one, people can continue to invest in their businesses, uh, without borrowing. Two, there's a broad range of, uh, organizations that can lend to help a business grow. And there are many businesses today that in their early stages, banks aren't the right place, uh, to support them anyway because of the risk and return, uh, that that represents.

So startups is a good example of that. And so I think that banks need to have that commercial decision making 'cause we are commercial organizations. But I also think to your balance point, that there is a role that the regulators can and do play. We have the conduct of financial institutions bill as you know.

Um, if there is a concern that says that banks are overly uh, rapidly exiting, uh, industries, then that can be addressed, uh, through giving a steer to the regulators. We don't think that the additional compliance costs required with the construct of this bill, uh, actually hit that trade off of cost benefit analysis.

Thank you. Uh, and I guess some of the other members of the committee will be more familiar with the way in which that works, but, uh, it'd be helpful for me anyway. Uh, if you could just, uh, spell out how that, uh, direction to regulators might, uh, what it might look like, uh, if, uh, government were concerned that an industry was, um, you know, seeing a lot of financial institutions walking away from supporting it.

Um, so one, I think the regulators, uh, would probably be far better to answer that question, uh, than I would. But certainly what I've um, seen is that a clear steer from the government sometimes to a regulator, uh, does result in changes from an industry perspective. Um, so I, I think there are many mechanisms, uh, at the government's, uh, behest, uh, if they've got a concern that they want to address, but we don't see the systemic, uh, concerns that the bill is trying to address.
Thank you. Thanks. We've got a question from David Wilson.

Are you just on mute there, David? Oh, you're frozen. Can you hear me? Yep. Yes. Um, Catherine, very pleased to hear that you're addressing access to banking services. Um, our research has uncovered 50,000 approximately in New Zealand that are in that situation.

So thank you for doing that. Um, I suppose probably one of the interesting conundrums we have here is that essentially ESG is a modern governance tool and it's relatively recent in terms of its approach and how you determine, as you and a number of others have said, risk.

Um, I, I suppose the problem we've got in some ways is, you know, the, the headline one was around, um, petrol stations and there's a, you know, flagging that they may be gradually removed from frontline banking services, including lending, um, given this ESG kind of governance approach.

But isn't it true that perhaps, um, petrol stations, for example, maybe in the future selling hydrogen, um, and that we are making a judgment now that is cutting off that future. And the other one I would throw in, uh, a couple I'd throw at you would be gold mining. You know, this is controversial.

Um, be members on this panel, they'll be dead against it and others that will be pro. Um, and I guess the question there is who decides? And, um, as you know, all banks have a social license to operate. So how do you kind of differentiate between those two things and make this workable for the country?

Uh, so let me start with petrol stations 'cause I think that's a great example. Um, and I confirm that we both continue to bank petrol stations and we have been taking on some new petrol stations. Um, but you need to look at them all in a case by case basis. And so an easy example is if I'm looking at a petrol station in Central Auckland where circa 50% of all new cars are eves, we'd want to have a conversation with that petrol station to say, if you look at your lending in the future and your ability to meet those repayments,

what plan have you got in place to ensure that as your petrol sales go down, that you've got other forms of cash flow that are going to come up, whether that's hydrogen or using the forecourt for a different purpose. Um, if I'm looking at a petrol station in another part of the country where the adoption of vehicles for very understandable reasons is longer off, then there's a potentially different transition risks that we need to look at with them.

And so our view is that the EESG assessments are a really important part of understanding future looking risks. And our job as a bank is to work with our customers to say, what's the best way that you can make sure your business is resilient in the future? And we take the same approach, whether it's a petrol station, whether it's a gold mine, or whether it's another type of business where there are some future cash flow challenges that we need to work with our customers to help make sure that they've thought them through so they end up profitable

for generations to come. Just a quick follow up. Thank you for that, Catherine. I guess what you are saying is that, um, the financial considerations that, uh, are thrown out in that process are what your primary concerns are because you talked about revenue and future revenue and how you're actually making business decisions, not the primary question around, I guess what this bill has been questioning itself is that you're making a decision purely on, uh, a governance, uh, approach, if you like, because trends in governance do vary and they do move.

And, you know, we may be considering very shortly nuclear energy, so you know, as a whole other raft of moral and financial issues that will throw themselves up. So I'm putting words in your mouth now, but I'm guess I'm saying that you are, uh, saying to us that your primary concern is the financial longevity of the business that you're dealing with.

Is that correct? Yes. My primary, primary focus is to make sure that our customers are resilient in the long term. And we're, for example, farmers. We know that 80% of our export markets have an interest in the providence of the goods that they have. I think it's really important that we work with our customers to make sure that their goods are going to be prime export goods again, for years to come and having those conversations and actually stepping in and helping to support.

And one of the ones that's been most interesting for me, we launched the Sustainable Farm Loan, uh, a couple of years ago. Over 45% of our lending is now on that sustainable farm loan. So that's saying 45% of all of our agri debt. Um, farmers are saying that they want to sign up to their own emissions, uh, uh, at their own carbon intensity reduction plan.

They set the targets themselves, we work with them on it. Um, and I think it's a great thing about helping New Zealands to be incredibly viable and relevant in the future. Thank you, Catherine. I Suppose what some of the people have said to us is that David Okay, sorry, was a supplementary. We have met time, but Catherine really appreciate you coming in.
Apologies and your written and oral submission. Thank you. Um, we are gonna move now to Sarah Walters. Sarah, you're up there.

Good morning. Can everyone hear me okay? Welcome. Perfect. We've got you for five minutes, but please allow time for questions at the end of your submission. Okay, thank you. I believe that's a term that's easily attributable to this proposed amendment. Believing this bill is fitting for a first world country. When this legislation more closely resembles that of a despotic dictatorship, and rather than leaving the decision making where it ought to be, this bill has the opposite effect in taking it away from the financial institutions being able to make their own decisions.

Access to banking services and facilities may be fundamental, but in a democracy, the right for an organization to exist must be within the mandate given by society. And in referring to your previous submitters, you talked about resilience. An organization is not resilient if it doesn't have the mandate to operate in society.

This bill is not the work of a sensible, well-functioning government calling a general policy statement referring to good people, childish as an insult to children, but seems fitting for the level of maturity shown. I wondered what a good person might be. So I entered good person into my weep browser without the time to run through the 15 traits listed.

I will quote the summary. Ultimately, it's up to the individual to determine what they feel are the qualities of a good person. Clause's 4, 4, 6 Ja does not recognize that commerce only exists within an environment and a culture not the other way round in a democratic New Zealand, this should not be determined by the government, but by the society in which the organization exists.

Clause 4, 4, 6, JB does not recognize that every organization should have choice in who it wishes to interact with as a customer and not be penalized for the decision it makes for the reasons that we're given in this bill. My submission is that this bill be withdrawn in its entirety as inappropriate and unnecessary interference from the government in the market.

It's completely with out of step with what a first world democratic country should be. Ultimately, it's up to the financial institutions themselves to determine what they feel are the qualities of a good person and decide who their customers are. Thank you, Uh, appreciate that. We've got a question from Andy Foster.

Yeah, Sarah, look, thank you. Thank you for your submission. Um, interesting comment you made about who determines the, um, you said at the end there ultimately should be up to the financial institution to make its own decisions. But we also see this whole thing of social license. We see a lot of times that, um, in this case, financial institutions, but sometimes other, um, uh, business organizations are put under a lot of pressure by part of society.

I won't say all of society, and we never know quite, uh, or very rarely, no, quite how much a society thinks that particular way. What's the, but if, if you take to a logical extent where you were going, then surely banks should also be immune from that sort of social and social pressure, which we sometimes see protest against banks, against banks for their decision making.

Any thoughts on that? I, I think that, um, society needs to, um, give license to any organization to operate and them showing their frustration or their unhappiness or their happiness with an organization and, and who they choose to support or not, um, is, is a good democracy in action.

Um, you, the banks themselves can process the information they're given from their various different sources and determine what the appropriate action is in that time. Uh, I don't believe it's up to the government to interfere. Um, traditionally governments only go where the people have already gone.

And by putting this barrier in place, it locks in a time and a place which may not have any relevance in the future. It's, it stops things from developing and changing as time goes on. Thank you, Sarah. We have met time. Appreciate you making your submission with, with us this morning.
Okay. Thank you. Uh, we are now gonna go to Iman Fraser.

You have my written thoughts and I'll take them as read.

Um, so instead I'm gonna be talking about what, um, what this bill, uh, actually is and, and what it's not. Um, so I guess my first thoughts, um, and I'm coming at this very much from the perspective of a customer of these organizations, is that this bill is presented as a shield for fairness.

Um, but it's really not, uh, it's a legislative demand that the banks kind of ignore social reality. Um, it's kind of, it's about dismissing, considering climate risk, social governance factors as, uh, murky moralizing, um, and as a citizen, as a customer of a bank, um, I find that view quite terrifying.

Um, risk is not a moral preference. It's a financial reality. When a bank assesses, an industry's business model is becoming obsolete in a changing world, that's not a political statement, it's a prudent commercial decision. Um, it's the kind of decision that predicts my savings. Uh, your mortgage, uh, and our economy stability.

Um, to legally compel a bank to ignore these, uh, risks is, is to compel them to make bad loans. Uh, it's asking financial guardians to close eyes and hope for the best. Uh, it's not a strategy to dereliction duty. Um, but I think it's worse than that as well, um, because, uh, this kind of legislation, uh, in an attempt to kind of remain neutral actually forces these organizations into becoming potentially unwilling partners of unethical practices.

Um, so considering the clause that forbids refusing service based on a client's industry, um, as was raised in the house, it can force a bank to continue servicing an entity, um, that may have had history of labor exploitation. It could force a bank to enable a business that has been incredibly accused of laundering money, uh, evading international sanctions.

Uh, and I'm aware that there's clauses, uh, around this, uh, for discretionary cases, but I think discretion here is, is a very, very dangerous, uh, tool. These are the people who are really locked outta the financial system. So I come to you today with a very primary and simple recommendation that's to abandon this bill. Um, I'm not a financial, uh, uh, and by no, by no means is this an area of my, my expertise, but excuse me. Um, and on one hand you've got, um, a prohibition from considering, uh, certain factors. And it's a good thing that we would, uh, change the law to provide that. In as much as there is a legitimate concern about financial institutions, um, overreaching and basically policing people's, uh, rights to their, um, policing people's rights to their free expression, I would support including political opinion within the, uh, within the protected grounds if the remainder of the bill targeting ESG guidelines and other sustainability considerations were to be dropped.

Um, I'd like to say that, uh, for that, especially for us young people, but it really should be for everyone that climate is also an issue of conscience.

Providing funding for these projects is both socially irresponsible, uh, and bad business sense in light of the damage that they cause our economy, If this bill were to pass, it would do incalculable damage to our hard one international reputation as a socially and environmentally responsible place to do business.

This is one of our competitive advantages and not something that we should be discarding lightly. Climate policy is difficult. It's, it's natural that different governments have different approaches and choose to make different trade offs based on values.
